flashcards.back: A programming paradigm that organizes code around 'objects' containing data (attributes) and behavior (methods). It models real-world entities as software objects.

flashcards.front: What is a Class in OOP?
flashcards.back: A blueprint or template that defines the properties (attributes) and behaviors (methods) for objects. It is a user-defined data type.

flashcards.front: What is an Object in OOP?
flashcards.back: An instance of a class. It is a real-world entity with identity, state (data), and behavior (methods). Objects occupy memory.
The bundling of data and methods into a single unit (class). It restricts direct access to some components, preventing accidental modification of data.
Hiding complex implementation details and showing only essential features of an object. It reduces complexity and improves usability.
A mechanism where a new class (derived class) inherits properties and behaviors from an existing class (base class). It promotes code reusability.
The ability of objects to take many forms. It allows one interface to be used for different data types (compile-time and runtime polymorphism).

flashcards.front: What was the first OOP language?
flashcards.back: Simula (1967), developed by Ole-Johan Dahl and Kristen Nygaard at Norwegian Computing Center. It introduced classes and inheritance.
Smalltalk, developed at Xerox PARC by Alan Kay. It was the first pure object-oriented language with dynamic typing.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Class and Object?
flashcards.back: Class is a blueprint/template; Object is an instance of a class. Class occupies no memory; Object occupies memory when created.

flashcards.front: What are the four pillars of OOP?
What is a Method in OOP?
flashcards.back: A function defined inside a class that describes the behavior of objects. It operates on object data (attributes).
A special method called automatically when an object is created. It initializes the object's state/attributes.
A special method called automatically when an object is destroyed. It performs cleanup operations like releasing memory.

flashcards.front: Why is OOP better than Procedural Programming?
flashcards.back: OOP provides better code organization, reusability (via inheritance), maintainability, modularity, and models real-world scenarios more naturally.
